But the worst charge made against
President Bryan is the charge that he
Is heart and soul in the proposition
to obliterate the word '"Agriculturar'
from the name of his classic institu-
ton. In open meeting President
Bryan has not stated his position,
but it Is told about the capitol that
he objects to the name containing
Agricultural" as something often
sive to the dillitanteism of such as
would send their children to the insti
of the courses offered by a most ex-
cellent institution along other lines
than those connected with bucolic
pursuits. In fact, the term "Agri-
cultural" is smacking of ruralism and
hay-forks and perspiration and soiled
raiment and fertilizer which is OBJ
noxious to a druggist of Kettle Falls





and who desires to take advantage
of state money as a factor in edu-
cation. Sweat and CRIME does not
bar any aspiring electrician student.
Odor of stables does not bar any man
who wants to become a veterinary
student. The sentiment here is that
President Bryan feels that any insti
-, 4 rri IL
turaP sent into the masonry of it's
name will not draw the students of
namby-pambyism who desires a di-
ploma to hang up in his sanctum as
an index of his attainments. In the
presidents mind there seems to be
a woful lack of language to cover
with exactitude the excellent null
tifarious objects for which his insti-
tuition was designed and created by
the people f a great state. Oh. for
word which would bring in more
than the idea conveyed by "polytech-
MIC" poly-anything-els so long as 1t
does not portray evidence or contact
with that ancient and honorable call
ing of getting an honorable living
from the forces OF nature when are
handed out more lavishly by nature
in the soil of the state, and, worst /
of all, the attempt to relegate the
word to the purlieus of polite society
comes from section of the state
whose glory is it's magnificent wheat
crops and whose wealth lies in the
unfastidious work of accepting ma
lures sol-gifts. Now, this may seem
extremely unkind to President Bryan,
but how unkind Is President Bryan
to the idea of "Agriculture?' From
the days when old Cincinnatus turned
his sword into ploughshare down
to the days of Enoch A. Bryan, with
a few exceptions, notable for their
dilletanteism, the farmer has been
recognized as the backbone OF any ma
ton or state worth mentioning.
